content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 20, 1998


Bradley W. Sullivan
City Attorney
City of Sutter Creek
18 Main Street
Post Office Box 366
Sutter Creek, California 95685

Re: Your Request for Advice
    Our File No. A-98-252

Dear Mr. Sullivan:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Sutter Creek City
Councilmember Randall E. Aspinall regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹

     Please note that other conflict of interest laws beyond the Political Reform Act may apply
to your facts, such as Government Code section 1090.² The Commission has no authority to
advise public officials about the application of laws outside the Act. (Section 83111.)

QUESTION

     Must Councilmember Aspinall refrain from making, participating in making or using his
position to influence the city's decisions regarding a waste haulage and garbage contract if
Amador County Environmental Services, Inc. is a potential bidder?


________________________
     ¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

     ² The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act and Government Code sections 1090
et seq. are two distinct statutory schemes. You may contact the Attorney General's Office for information regarding
Government Code sections 1090 et seq.

File No. A-98-252
Page 2

ANSWER

Yes. Amador County Environmental Services, Inc. ("ACES") is a source of income to
Councilmember Aspinall. It is reasonably foreseeable that awarding or not awarding the service
contract to ACES will have a financial impact on that business entity. Therefore, Mr. Aspinall
must refrain from making, participating in making or otherwise using his official position to
influence this governmental decision.

FACTS

Randall E. Aspinall is a member of the Sutter Creek City Council. Councilmember
Aspinall is also the president of The Aspinall Company ("Aspinall Co."), a company that
provides professional business consulting in the areas of public relations, marketing and
advertising.

On December 27, 1997, ACES contracted with Aspinall Co. to obtain a franchise garbage
contract with the City of Jackson (adjacent to Sutter Creek). After successfully obtaining the
franchise, ACES continued to retain the services of Aspinall Co. On the average, Aspinall Co.
billed ACES and Sierra Septic (an ACES subsidiary) approximately $400 per month. Aspinall
Co. terminated its services to ACES on September 4, 1998.

On August 3, 1998, after you researched the various franchises within Sutter Creek, you
determined that the waste haulage and garbage franchise with Amador Disposal Services, Inc.
("ADS") had expired, and that new bids for the franchise needed to be submitted. The city is
currently soliciting bids for the garbage franchise contract. ACES is one of two competing
bidders for the contract.

Councilmember Aspinall does not have any ownership interest in ACES, Sierra Septic, or
any related entities. The councilmember's compensation was on an hourly basis, and there was
no bonus or contingency-type arrangement contemplated or in effect.

You have asked whether Councilmember Aspinall must refrain from making,
participating in making or using his position to influence the city's decisions regarding the waste
haulage and garbage contract.3

3 Your letter indicates that Councilmember Aspinall has to this point disqualified himself from taking any
actions regarding the waste haulage and garbage contract. Your letter specifically asks if the city is prohibited from
contracting with ACES under Government Code section 1090 even though Mr. Aspinall has refrained from
participating on this issue. Again, we cannot answer this question. This letter only addresses Mr. Aspinall's duties
under the Political Reform Act.

File No. A-98-252
Page 3

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act provides that no public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the official knows or has reason to know
he or she has a financial interest. (Regulation 18700(a)).⁴ As a member of the city council,
Mr. Aspinall is a public official for purposes of the Act. (Section 82048.)

A public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five
enumerated interests including any source of income aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more within a twelve (12) month period. (Section 87103(c).) ACES paid Mr. Aspinall
approximately $400 per month for consulting services through September 4, 1998. Therefore,
ACES is a source of income to Councilmember Aspinall. As a result, he may not make,
participate in making or use his official position to influence a decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on ACES.

The next step in the analysis is to evaluate whether it is "reasonably foreseeable" that
awarding or not awarding the waste haulage and garbage contract to ACES will have a material
financial effect on ACES. The answer depends on whether ACES is directly or indirectly
involved in the decision. (Regulations 18700(b)(4).) Regulation 18704.1 provides that a source
of income is directly involved in a decision before an agency if the decision involves a contract
with the source of income. (Regulation 18704.1(a)(2).) ACES is directly involved in this
particular decision.

Regulation 18705.3 sets forth the materiality standard when the source of income is
directly involved in a decision. It provides that "[a]ny reasonably foreseeable financial effect on
a person who is a source of income to a public official, and who is directly involved in a decision
before the official's agency, is deemed material." It is certainly reasonably foreseeable that
awarding or not awarding the service contract to ACES will have a financial impact on that
business entity. Therefore, Councilmember Aspinall has a conflict and must refrain from making
or participating in making this decision. (Regulations 18702.1 and 18702.2.) He must also
refrain from contacting, appearing before, or otherwise attempting to influence any public
official regarding this decision. (Regulation 18702.3.)

⁴ On October 1, 1998, the Commission approved a reorganization of the existing conflict-of-interest
regulations in order to make those regulations easier to understand and apply, without changing their substance.
This reorganization repeals former Regulations 18700-18720, and incorporates their substance into new Regulations
18700-18708. The Commission is now basing its advice on the new regulations. Copies of the new regulations are
enclosed with this letter.

File No. A-98-252
Page 4

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

Deborah allison/ts

By: Deborah L. Allison
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
